html:
III

Charity

A dramatic event occurred that same afternoon at the shop. Violet and Henry were together in the office, where the electricity had just been turned on; the shop itself was still depending on nature for light, and lay somewhat obscure in the dusk. Husband and wife were in an affectionate mood, for Violet as usual had been beaten by the man‚Äôs extraordinary soft obstinacy. She had had more than one scene of desperation with him about his health and his treatment of himself, but nobody can keep on fighting a cushion forever. Henry had worn her down into a good temper, into a condition of reassurance and even optimism. He had, in fact, by patience convinced her that his indisposition was temporary and such as none can hope to escape; and that he undoubtedly possessed a constitution of iron. The absence of Elsie helped the intimacy of the pair; they enjoyed being alone, unobserved, free from the constraint of the eyes of a third person who was here, there and everywhere. The trouble was that as soon as the affectionate mood had been established Violet wanted to begin her tactics and her antics all over again.

‚ÄúYou know, darling,‚Äù she said, playful and serious, sitting on the edge of the desk by his side in a manner most unmatronly. ‚ÄúEither you eat tomorrow, or I shall have the doctor in. Oh! I shall have the doctor in! It‚Äôs for you to decide, but I‚Äôve made up my mind. You must admit‚ÅÝ‚Äî‚Äù

And then the shop door opened and someone entered. Violet sprang off the desk to the switches, illuminated the shop, and beheld Dr.¬ÝRaste. Henry also beheld Dr.¬ÝRaste. Although a perfectly innocent woman, Violet‚Äôs face at once changed to that of a wicked conspirator who has been caught in the act. Try as she would she could not get rid of that demeanour of guilt, and the more she tried the less she succeeded. She dared not look at Henry. Certainly she could not murmur to Henry; ‚ÄúI swear to you I didn‚Äôt send for him. His coming‚Äôs just as much a surprise to me as it is to you.‚Äù She thought: ‚ÄúThis is that girl Elsie‚Äôs doing.‚Äù And she was angry and resentful against Elsie, and yet timorously glad that Elsie had been interfering. What Henry was thinking no one could guess. Henry‚Äôs mind to him a kingdom was, and a kingdom never invaded. All that could be positively stated of Henry was that the moment he recognized the doctor he rose vigorously from his chair and limped about with vivacity to prove that he was not an invalid, or in any way in need of any doctor. And, strange to say, he really felt quite well. Dr.¬ÝRaste startled Violet by offering to shake hands.

‚ÄúHa! How d‚Äôye do, Mrs.¬ÝEarlforward,‚Äù said he, in his sprightly, professional, high-voiced style. ‚ÄúNot seen you for a long time.‚Äù

Violet recalled the Sunday morning in Riceyman Square when he had spoken to Henry on the pavement. She was happy then, and expectant of happiness. She was girlish then, exuberant, dominating, self-willed, free. None could withstand her. A year ago! The change in twelve months suddenly presented itself to her with a sinister significance; but she imagined that the change was confined to her circumstances, and that an unchanged Violet had survived.

The doctor with his fresh eyes saw a shrunken woman, subject to some kind of neurosis which he could not diagnose. He greeted the oncoming Mr.¬ÝEarlforward, and shook a hand of parchment. Mr.¬ÝEarlforward‚Äôs appearance indeed astonished him, and he said to himself that perhaps he had done well to call, and that anyhow Elsie had not exaggerated her report, Mr.¬ÝEarlforward was worse than shrunken‚ÅÝ‚Äîhe was emaciated; his jaws were hollowed, his little eyes had receded, his complexion was greyish, his lips were pale and dry‚ÅÝ‚Äîthe lower lip had lost its heavy fullness; his ears were nearly white. And there he was moving nervously about in the determination to be in excellent health in the presence of the doctor. Amazing, thought Dr.¬ÝRaste, that Mrs.¬ÝEarlforward had not summoned medical assistance weeks earlier! But then Mrs.¬ÝEarlforward saw her husband every day and nearly all day. Amazing that no customer had dropped a word of alarm! But then Mr.¬ÝEarlforwaid‚Äôs amiable and bland relations with customers were not such as to permit any kind of intimacy. You got a certain distance with Mr.¬ÝEarlforward, but you never got any further.

‚ÄúYou remember I bought a Shakespeare here last year,‚Äù Dr.¬ÝRaste began cheerily, and somewhat loudly. (He often spoke more loudly than he need: result of imposing himself on the resistant stupidity of the proletariat.) Relief spread through the shop like a sweet odour. The professional man‚Äôs visit was a pure coincidence after all. Violet ceased to look guilty. Henry ceased to ape the person of vigorous health.

‚ÄúYes, yes,‚Äù said Mr.¬ÝEarlforward; and to his wife: ‚ÄúJust reach down that ‚ÄòShakespeare with Illustrations,‚Äô will you?‚Äù

‚ÄúShakespeare with Illustrations‚Äù was the shop‚Äôs title for the work (Valpy‚Äôs edition of Shakespeare‚Äôs plays and poems), because these three words were the only words on the binding.

‚ÄúYou don‚Äôt mean to say you‚Äôve not sold it yet‚ÅÝ‚Äîa year, isn‚Äôt it?‚Äù cried Dr.¬ÝRaste.

And Mr.¬ÝEarlforward recalled from their previous interview in the shop an impression that the doctor was apt to be impudent. What right had the man to express surprise at the work not having been sold? Mr.¬ÝEarlforward had in stock books bought ten years ago, fifteen years ago.

‚ÄúI could have sold it,‚Äù said he. ‚ÄúBut the truth is I‚Äôve been keeping it for you. I felt sure you‚Äôd be looking in one of these days. I meant to drop you a postcard to say I‚Äôd found it; but somehow‚ÅÝ‚Äî‚Äù

All this was true. For at least ten months Mr.¬ÝEarlforward had intended to drop the postcard, and had never dropped it. Yet his conviction that one day he would drop it had remained fresh and strong throughout the period.

‚ÄúHere! It‚Äôs up in that corner, my dear,‚Äù said Mr.¬ÝEarlforward.

‚ÄúYes, I know. I‚Äôm just going to get the steps.‚Äù

‚ÄúWhere are they? They ought to be here.‚Äù

‚ÄúI don‚Äôt know. Elsie must have had them for her windows, and forgotten to bring them back.‚Äù

‚ÄúTut, tut!‚Äù Mr.¬ÝEarlforward blandly expostulated.

‚ÄúShakespeare‚Äôs been having considerable success in my house,‚Äù Dr.¬ÝRaste went on, when the two men were alone, with an arch smile at his own phrasing. ‚ÄúYou‚Äôd scarcely believe it, but my little daughter simply devours him. And as it‚Äôs her birthday next week I thought I‚Äôd give her my Globe edition for herself, and get another one with a wee bit larger type for myself. My eyes aren‚Äôt what they were.‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ Simply devours him! Scarcely believe it, would you?‚Äù The doctor was growing human. His eyes sparkled with ingenuous paternal pride. Then he checked himself.

‚ÄúI notice your old clock isn‚Äôt going,‚Äù said he, in a more conventional, a conversation-making tone, and glanced at his wrist.

‚ÄúNo,‚Äù Mr.¬ÝEarlforward quietly admitted, thinking: ‚ÄúWhat‚Äôs it got to do with you‚ÅÝ‚Äîmy ‚Äòold clock‚Äô not going?‚Äù The clock had not gone for months.

Violet, who had further illuminated the shop as she passed out, was rather long in returning, partly because she had had to hunt for the steps, and partly because she had popped into the bedroom to see that it was in order. Dr.¬ÝRaste gallantly took the volumes from her as she stood halfway up the steps.

‚ÄúFifteen volumes‚ÅÝ‚Äîthat‚Äôs right,‚Äù said Mr.¬ÝEarlforward. ‚ÄúI told you there were eight, didn‚Äôt I?‚Äù

‚ÄúDid you?‚Äù said Dr.¬ÝRaste, wondering at the bookseller‚Äôs memory.

‚ÄúYes. I was mixing it up with another edition. Easy to make a mistake of that kind. Well, just look at it. Biography. Notes. Beautiful clear type. Nice, modest binding, in very good taste. Light and handy to hold. Clean as a pin. Nearly two hundred illustrations‚ÅÝ‚Äîfrom the Boydell edition. I told you Flaxman‚Äôs illustrations, didn‚Äôt I? Yes, I did. That was wrong. I somehow got the idea they were Flaxman‚Äôs because they‚Äôre in outline. But I see there‚Äôs quite a selection of artists.‚Äù He peered at the names engraved in microscopic characters under the illustrations, and passed on volume after volume to the prospective customer. ‚ÄúPretty edition.‚Äù

A silence. Violet stood attendant‚ÅÝ‚Äîan acolyte, submissive, watchful‚ÅÝ‚Äîwhile Henry did business.

‚ÄúI‚Äôm afraid it‚Äôll be too dear for my purse,‚Äù said the doctor, affrighted by the thought of nearly two hundred illustrations from Boydell.

‚ÄúTwenty-five shillings.‚Äù

‚ÄúI‚Äôd better take it,‚Äù said the doctor, looking up from the books into Mr.¬ÝEarlforward‚Äôs little eyes; he was startled at the lowness of the price, and immediately counted out the money‚ÅÝ‚Äîtwo notes and two new half-crowns, which Mr.¬ÝEarlforward gazed at passionately, and in a bravura of self-control left lying on the desk.

‚ÄúMake them up into two parcels, will you?‚Äù said the doctor. ‚ÄúI‚Äôll carry them home myself. I suppose you wouldn‚Äôt be able to deliver tonight? Too late?‚Äù

‚ÄúYes. Too late tonight, I‚Äôm afraid,‚Äù answered Mr.¬ÝEarlforward calmly, well aware that he had long since ceased to deliver any goods under any circumstances. ‚ÄúMy dear, some nice brown paper and string. Oh! The string‚Äôs here, isn‚Äôt it?‚Äù He bent down to a drawer of the desk, and drew out a tangle of all manner of pieces of string.

Violet now became important in the episode, and took charge of the wrapping; her mien showed a conviction that she could make up a parcel as well as her husband.

‚ÄúHospitals are getting in a bad way,‚Äù said Dr.¬ÝRaste, and Mr.¬ÝEarlforward thought to himself that the doctor was one of those distressing persons who from nervousness could not endure a silence.

‚ÄúYes?‚Äù

‚ÄúYes. Haven‚Äôt you read about it in the papers?‚Äù

‚ÄúWell, I may have seen something about it,‚Äù said Mr.¬ÝEarlforward. But he had not seen anything about it, nor did he care anything about it. He held the common view that hospitals were maintained by magic, or if not by magic, then by the cheques of millionaires in great houses in the West End who paid subscriptions as they paid their rates and taxes.

‚ÄúYes. The London Hospital‚ÅÝ‚Äîour largest hospital‚ÅÝ‚Äîunparalleled work in the East End, you know‚ÅÝ‚Äîthe London‚Äôs thinking of closing a hundred beds. A calamity, but there seems to be no alternative. My wife‚Äôs interesting herself in Lord Knutsford‚Äôs special effort to save the beds; she used to be on the staff. I was just wondering whether you‚Äôd care to give me something for her list.‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ I thought I might mention it‚ÅÝ‚Äîas I‚Äôm not here professionally. Here as a customer, you see.‚Äù He gave one of his little, nervous laughs.

Mr.¬ÝEarlforward perceived that the doctor had not been merely breaking a silence. He perceived also that Violet, mysteriously excited by the name of the legendary subscription-collecting peer who directed the London Hospital, was ‚Äúwilling‚Äù him to practise charity on this occasion. He keenly regretted, as the doctor developed his subject, that he had left the price of the Shakespeare on the desk. There it lay, waiting to be given, asking to be given! There it lay and could not be ignored. The doctor was, of course, being impudent again; but there the money lay. Half a crown? Too little. Two half-crowns, those bright and lovely objects? Too little‚ÅÝ‚Äîor at any rate too little so long as the notes lay beside them. A note? Impossible! Fantastic! The situation was desperate, and Mr.¬ÝEarlforward in agony. He could not in decency refuse‚ÅÝ‚Äîhe a Londoner, fond of London and its institutions‚ÅÝ‚Äîhe an established tradesman; neither could he part with his money. He was about to martyrize himself; his hand, each finger separately suffering, hovered over one of the notes, when deliverance occurred to him.

‚ÄúI‚Äôll tell you what I‚Äôll do,‚Äù said he, and picked up a thin, tattered, quarto volume that was lying on the desk. ‚ÄúI‚Äôll make you a sporting offer. Here‚Äôs one of the earliest collected editions of Gray‚Äôs Poems.‚Äù

‚ÄúGray? Gray?‚Äù reflected the doctor, and aloud: ‚Äú‚Ää‚ÄòElegy in a Country Churchyard‚Äô sort of thing?‚Äù

‚ÄúYes. This is the Glasgow edition, and I can‚Äôt remember now whether it or the London edition was the first‚ÅÝ‚Äîthe first collected edition, I mean. They are both dated 1768. I‚Äôll give you this for your hospital. You take it to Sotherans or Bain, and see what it‚Äôll fetch.‚Äù

The doctor opened the book.

‚Äú‚Ää‚ÄòFull many a flower is born to blush unseen
And waste its sweetness on the desart air.‚Äô‚Ää‚Äù

he read. ‚ÄúFunny way of spelling ‚Äòdesert,‚Äô a, r, t. But this is very interesting. ‚ÄòFull many a flower‚ÅÝ‚Äî‚Äô So that‚Äôs Gray, is it? Very interesting.‚Äù He was quite uplifted by the sight of familiar words in an old book. ‚ÄúIt‚Äôs very clean inside. Suppose it‚Äôs worth a lot of money. I‚Äôm sure you‚Äôre very generous, very generous indeed.‚Äù Violet paused in making up the second parcel.

‚ÄúWell,‚Äù said Mr.¬ÝEarlforward, uplifted in his turn by reason of the epithet ‚Äúgenerous‚Äù applied to him. ‚ÄúI don‚Äôt know without inquiring just what it is worth. That‚Äôs the sporting offer.‚Äù

‚ÄúI wouldn‚Äôt mind giving a couple of pounds for it myself. I should like it.

‚Äú‚Ää‚ÄòFar from the madding crowd‚ÅÝ‚Äî,‚Äô

‚ÄúWell, well! And one of the earlier editions, you say?‚Äù

‚ÄúNot earliest of the Elegy. Earliest of the collected poems.‚Äù

‚ÄúJust so! Just so! Two pounds a fair price?‚Äù

‚ÄúI‚Äôm afraid it‚Äôs worth more than that, at the worst,‚Äù said Mr.¬ÝEarlforward, suddenly grieved. He saw to what an extent he was making a fool of himself‚ÅÝ‚Äîlosing pounds in order to save a ten-shilling note! Ridiculous! Idiotic! Mad! True, he had bought the book for ten shillings, and he strove to regard the transaction from the angle of his own disbursement. But he could not deny that he was losing pounds. Yes, pounds and pounds. Still, he could not have let the ten-shilling note go. A ten-shilling note was a treasure, whereas a book was only a book. Illogical, but instinct was more powerful than logic.

‚ÄúAh!‚Äù said the doctor. ‚ÄúIf it‚Äôs worth more than two pounds I must sell it. You‚Äôre generous. Mr.¬ÝEarlforward, you‚Äôre generous. Thank you.‚Äù

Violet rearranged the second parcel, including the Gray in it, while Dr.¬ÝRaste expanded further in gratitude.

‚ÄúThat type won‚Äôt strain anybody‚Äôs eyes,‚Äù Mr.¬ÝEarlforward commented on the Gray as it disappeared within brown paper.

‚ÄúNo.‚Äù

‚ÄúI‚Äôm thankful to say my eyesight doesn‚Äôt give me any trouble now.‚Äù

‚ÄúUm!‚Äù said the doctor, gazing at the bookseller, and taking the chance to feel his way towards the matter which had brought him into the shop. ‚ÄúI shouldn‚Äôt say you were looking quite the man you were when I saw you last.‚Äù

‚ÄúNo, he is not!‚Äù Violet put in eagerly.

‚ÄúOh! I‚Äôm all right,‚Äù Mr.¬ÝEarlforward, defending himself against yet another example of the doctor‚Äôs impudence. ‚ÄúAll I want is more exercise, and I can‚Äôt get that because of my knee, you know.‚Äù

‚ÄúYes,‚Äù said the doctor. ‚ÄúI‚Äôve always noticed you limp. You ought to go to Barker. I shouldn‚Äôt be surprised if he could put you right in ten minutes. Not a qualified man, of course; but wonderful cures!‚ÅÝ‚Ää‚ÅÝ‚Ä¶ You might never limp again.‚Äù

‚ÄúBut he charges very heavy, doesn‚Äôt he? I‚Äôve heard of fifty pounds.‚Äù

‚ÄúI don‚Äôt know. Supposing he does? Well worth it, isn‚Äôt it, to be cured? What‚Äôs money?‚Äù

Mr.¬ÝEarlforward made no reply to this silly question. Fifty pounds, or anything like it, for just pulling your knee about! ‚ÄúWhat was money,‚Äù indeed! He seized the money on the table. The doctor understood himself to have been definitely repulsed. Being a philosopher, he felt resigned. He had done what he could at an expense of twenty-five shillings. He lodged one of the parcels under his left arm and he took the other in his left hand and assumed a demeanour, compulsory in a gentleman, to indicate to the world that the parcels were entirely without weight, and that he was carrying them out of caprice and not from necessity.

‚ÄúHere, doctor,‚Äù Violet most unexpectedly exclaimed. ‚ÄúAs you are here I think I‚Äôll consult you.‚Äù

‚ÄúNot about me! Not about me!‚Äù Mr.¬ÝEarlforward protested plaintively, imploringly, and yet implacably.

Violet leaned over him with an endearment.

‚ÄúNo, darling, not about you,‚Äù she cooed. ‚ÄúAbout myself.‚Äù

‚ÄúI didn‚Äôt know there was anything particular wrong with you.‚Äù

‚ÄúDidn‚Äôt you?‚Äù said Violet in a strange tone at once dry and affectionate. ‚ÄúElsie did. Will you come upstairs, doctor?‚Äù She was no longer the packer of books. She had initiative, authority, dominion. Horribly suspecting her duplicity, Henry watched her leave the office in front of the doctor, who had set down his parcels. Never, never, would he have a doctor!
